question: Using Table 1.3-2 and the text description of NuScale safety systems, compare the systems NuScale eliminates versus those retained.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate large-break LOCA scenarios and reduce reliance on external power, resulting in a lower core damage frequency and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0021.title: Engineered Safety Features and Emergency Systems
nuscale_ch01_0021.summary: The NuScale reactor maintains a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV) to reduce corrosion, facilitate leak detection, and minimize heat transfer and oxygen content, enhancing safety and reliability. In emergencies, passive systems rapidly reduce containment pressure and temperature without external power, and the integral design eliminates large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) risks, resulting in a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0128.title: 1.9.3 Generic Issues
nuscale_ch01_0128.summary: This section describes NuScale’s process for ensuring conformance with regulatory criteria, including a review of the Standard Review Plan (SRP), Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS), and NRC Interim Staff Guidance. It also outlines how NuScale addresses technically relevant Three Mile Island (TMI) requirements and unresolved or high-priority generic safety issues, referencing the NRC’s Generic Issues Program and relevant regulatory documents.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights differences in size, output, system components, and safety systems, showing that NuScale uses a smaller, simpler, and more integrated design with fewer external components compared to conventional PWRs. The comparison is summarized in tables for clarity.

nuscale_ch01_0076.title: Conformance with Standard Review Plan
nuscale_ch01_0076.summary: NuScale reviewed the Standard Review Plan (SRP), including all relevant guidance and acceptance criteria, and documented any gaps in a summary report submitted to the NRC. The company also assessed conformance with NRC-developed guidance specific to small modular reactors and addressed applicable Three Mile Island requirements and unresolved generic safety issues, as required by federal regulations. Relevant information and resolutions are incorporated into referenced tables and sections of the Final Safety Analysis Report.

nuscale_ch01_0558.title: DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0: Radiation Protection Design FeaturesII.9 RG 8.15
nuscale_ch01_0558.summary: This section summarizes the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report’s conformance with various regulatory guides related to radiation protection design features, as outlined in DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0. It details whether each regulatory guide is applicable, and if so, whether the design fully or partially conforms, with references to supporting sections or tables for further information. Several guides are noted as not applicable, while others are partially or fully met by the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The section describes how maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV) of the NuScale reactor provides several safety benefits, such as reducing corrosion, aiding leak detection, and limiting combustible gases. The NuScale design enhances safety through passive systems, a simplified integral design that eliminates large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) scenarios, and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors, resulting in a lower core damage frequency.

nuscale_ch01_0025.title: Control Room Habitability System
nuscale_ch01_0025.summary: The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive safety feature designed to protect plant operators from accidental releases of toxic or radioactive gases by supplying clean, compressed air to the main control room (MCR), even during power loss. It maintains positive pressure in protected areas and has enough air storage to provide breathable air for at least 72 hours after an incident.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment, condenses it, and recirculates water to cool the core, with heat ultimately conducted passively to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves—including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures, and in borated coolant—using a test setup that replicated key valve components and features such as the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility to ensure the valves met design and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0177.title: DSRS 6.6, Rev 0: Inservice Inspection and Testing of Class 2 and 3 ComponentsII.6 System Pressure Tests
nuscale_ch01_0177.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, specifically regarding system pressure tests. It confirms that NuScale meets all relevant requirements outlined in DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, and notes that certain SRP sections related to ice condenser and suppression pool systems are not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0817.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.11 D&S System extremely high probability of functioning in the event of an AOO.
nuscale_ch01_0817.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with DSRS 15.9.A requirements for thermal hydraulic stability. The NuScale design does not use detect and suppress methods or exclusion zones; instead, existing reactor trip signals act preemptively to prevent conditions that could lead to flow instabilities, ensuring high reliability during anticipated operational occurrences (AOO). Backup detect and suppress options and related instrumentation are not applicable, as the reactor trip system already provides the necessary protection.

nuscale_ch01_0448.title: SRP 10.4.9, Rev 3: Auxiliary Feedwater System (PWR)All Various
nuscale_ch01_0448.summary: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report states that the NuScale plant design does not require or use an auxiliary feedwater system, as its Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) fulfills similar functions but differs in design, operation, and response to small break LOCAs. Additionally, several regulatory criteria related to heat removal, inspection, and testing are deemed not applicable to NuScale’s condensate and feedwater systems, while partial or full conformance is noted for criteria on fluid instabilities, system sharing, and flow accelerated corrosion.

nuscale_ch01_0061.title: Control Rod Drive Mechanism Proof Test
nuscale_ch01_0061.summary: The NuScale Integral System Test Program uses the NIST-1 facility, a scaled model of the NuScale Power Module (NPM), to generate thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and validation of safety analysis codes such as NRELAP5 and PIM. These tests, conducted at Oregon State University, ensure that the unique design features and safety methodologies of the NPM are accurately represented and validated for licensing and operational stability.

nuscale_ch01_0060.title: NuScale Integral System Test Program
nuscale_ch01_0060.summary: The NuScale Integral System Test Program was established to collect thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and to validate safety analysis codes using a scaled model of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design. The NIST-1 facility replicates key reactor systems and supports the development and validation of proprietary safety codes, ensuring accurate modeling of the NPM’s unique features. Testing at NIST-1 has provided essential data for NuScale’s safety analysis and design certification efforts.

nuscale_ch01_0042.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch01_0042.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System section describes the digital control systems in the NuScale plant, emphasizing the independence between safety-related and nonsafety-related systems. Reactor operators use advanced, human-factored displays in the main control room to monitor and control each reactor module, with alarm management and supervisory functions integrated for effective plant operation. Additionally, the section mentions the Technical Support Center and the Radioactive Waste Building, which handles the processing and shipment of radioactive waste.
